message:
Add browser monotonic clock adapter and ready-handoff fairness bound

Implement a monotonic clock adapter for browser environments that
smooths Performance.now() jitter and handles visibility-change
suspensions, plus a runtime config knob to prevent browser pump
monopolization under adversarial ready floods.

src/time/driver.rs:
- Add BrowserClockConfig struct with max_forward_step and jitter_floor
  parameters controlling clock advancement behavior
- Add BrowserMonotonicClock struct using atomic state to provide a
  monotonic, jitter-smoothing, catch-up-capped time source suitable
  for browser tabs where Performance.now() can regress or jump
- Implement observe_host_time()/observe_host_nanos() for ingesting
  host time samples with first-sample rebase, regression clamping,
  jitter floor accumulation, and catch-up capping to prevent
  timer avalanches after background-tab resumption
- Implement suspend()/resume() for explicit visibility-change
  lifecycle management (pauses clock advancement during hidden tabs)
- Implement TimeSource trait for BrowserMonotonicClock
- Add TimerDriverHandle::with_browser_clock() constructor for wiring
  the browser clock into the existing timer infrastructure
- Add 7 tests: first sample rebase, regression clamping, jitter floor
  accumulation, catch-up cap enforcement, suspend/resume round-trip,
  timer driver integration, and handle integration

src/time/mod.rs:
- Re-export BrowserClockConfig and BrowserMonotonicClock

src/runtime/config.rs:
- Add browser_ready_handoff_limit field to RuntimeConfig (default 0,
  disabled) that caps consecutive ready-lane dispatches in
  single-thread browser pumps before yielding to the host event
  queue, preventing unbounded host-turn monopolization
- Add default table entry and test coverage for the new field across
  default, minimum, and custom config test cases

docs/wasm_browser_scheduler_semantics.md:
- Document the timer backend adaptation contract including monotonic
  clock adapter requirements, regression clamping semantics, jitter
  accumulation behavior, catch-up capping rationale, and
  suspend/resume lifecycle
- Add evidence artifact requirements and new repro commands

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -134,6 +134,15 @@ Purpose: preserve runtime progress while preventing UI starvation and perpetual
 - Condition: tab suspension or long host delay causes large timer catch-up.
 - Behavior: process catch-up in bounded batches, yielding between batches; do not violate fairness bound.
 
+Timer backend adaptation contract (`asupersync-umelq.5.2`) binds this to explicit policy:
+
+- Use a monotonic browser clock adapter fed by host samples (for example `performance.now()`).
+- First host sample after bootstrap or resume establishes baseline and does not jump runtime time.
+- Regressed host samples are clamped (runtime time never moves backward).
+- Small sub-floor jitter deltas are accumulated, then released deterministically.
+- Large forward deltas are capped per observation via `max_forward_step`, with remaining debt drained across subsequent observations.
+- Visibility suspend/resume must call clock suspend/resume so hidden-tab gaps do not explode timer deadlines in a single turn.
+
 ### F4 Capability Denial
 
 - Condition: missing/invalid authority for host callback path.
@@ -219,13 +228,21 @@ Required artifacts:
 - parity diff summary,
 - deterministic repro command block.
 
+For `asupersync-umelq.5.2`, artifacts must additionally include:
+
+- browser clock policy config (`max_forward_step`, `jitter_floor`),
+- deferred catch-up progression snapshot,
+- suspend/resume transition evidence.
+
 ## Reproduction Commands
 
 Use remote offload for cargo-heavy commands:
 
 ```bash
 rch exec -- cargo test --all-targets sched_browser -- --nocapture
 rch exec -- cargo test -p asupersync --features test-internals parity_browser_scheduler -- --nocapture
+rch exec -- cargo test -p asupersync browser_clock -- --nocapture
+rch exec -- cargo test -p asupersync timer_driver_with_browser_clock -- --nocapture
 rch exec -- cargo run --features cli --bin asupersync -- trace verify --strict artifacts/browser_scheduler.trace
 ```
